intro: Basis Theory and Nium both show up under the payments orchestration category, but they solve slightly different jobs. Basis Theory uses undisclosed pricing with free tier, while Nium runs on custom (sales-led) pricing. Lock-in is medium for Basis Theory and high for Nium. Transparency lands at 4/5 versus 1/5. Basis Theory fits teams working on tokenizing payment card data to reduce PCI DSS scope, while Nium is a closer match when the job is cross-border payouts to 190+ countries for gig platforms. Worth noting: Basis Theory is explicitly not for merchants wanting a full routing, reconciliation, and analytics product out of the box; Nium is explicitly not for consumer merchants looking primarily for smart card-routing across PSPs. The honest trade-off: Basis Theory trades off on tokenization only — no routing or acquiring; Nium trades off on positioned as infra, not merchant orchestration. On the plus side, Basis Theory highlights processor-agnostic tokens portable across any PSP, while Nium points to global payouts to 190+ countries and 100+ currencies.
quick_take: Basis Theory is for tokenizing payment card data to reduce; Nium is for cross-border payouts to 190+ countries for; decide on pricing model.
verdict_a: Choose Basis Theory if your project is tokenizing payment card data to reduce PCI DSS scope, medium lock-in is an acceptable trade-off.
verdict_b: Choose Nium if your project is cross-border payouts to 190+ countries for gig platforms, you are prepared for a sales-led custom-pricing conversation, you are willing to accept the high lock-in called out in our data.

faq.q: Is Basis Theory cheaper than Nium?
faq.a: Basis Theory uses undisclosed pricing with free tier, and Nium uses custom (sales-led) pricing. The pricing models are different, so a direct cheaper-than comparison depends on your volume and usage pattern. Basis Theory offers a free tier; Nium does not.

faq.q: Can I migrate from Basis Theory to Nium?
faq.a: Our data puts Basis Theory at medium lock-in, and Nium at high lock-in (global payments infra, deep integration). Migration is feasible but not trivial — budget time for re-integration, data export, and parallel running before cutover.
Both score 4/5 on developer experience in our data, so there's no clear winner on that axis. Basis Theory does edge ahead on pricing/docs transparency (4/5 vs 1/5), which can make evaluation faster.

faq.q: Is Nium a good alternative to Basis Theory?
faq.a: Nium is a reasonable alternative to Basis Theory when your workload leans more toward platforms, marketplaces, and fintechs needing cross-border payouts, cards, and FX via one licensed provider than engineering teams that want PCI offload and PSP portability without being locked into Stripe or Adyen vaults. The pricing model shifts too — Basis Theory is undisclosed pricing with free tier, Nium is custom (sales-led) pricing — so expect the cost profile to change as well. One caveat: Nium is explicitly not for consumer merchants looking primarily for smart card-routing across PSPs, so check that constraint against your use-case before switching.
